summary: Repository-wide hygiene PR that added a small shell script to ensure every tracked file ends with a final newline, then applied it across 139 files. The diff is almost entirely removal of Git's "No newline at end of file" markers in headers, C sources, assembly, docs, and config files, with no semantic code changes evident.

key_files.why: New helper script that scans tracked files and appends a newline when the last byte is not readable as a newline.
Root config normalized to end with a final newline.
Root metadata file normalized to end with a final newline.
Documentation normalized to end with a final newline.
Representative public header fixed only for final newline.
Representative assembly file fixed only for final newline.
Representative fighter source fixed only for final newline.

what_changed: The script runs over git-tracked files using `git ls-files -z` and a null-delimited read loop.
For each tracked file, the script checks the final byte with `tail -c1 <"$f" | read -r _` and appends an empty line with `echo >>"$f"` when needed.
Ran the script across the repository, changing 139 files with 143 added lines and 134 deleted lines according to the PR context.
Most file changes are one-line diff artifacts where the content stayed the same but Git's `\ No newline at end of file` marker disappeared.
An empty tracked assembly file, `asm/melee/ft/ftlib.s`, became a one-newline file.
Affected files span root config, docs, assembly, public headers, MSL sources, Dolphin/GX/OS headers, sysdolphin baselib headers, and fighter source/header files.
smart_moves: The fix was automated instead of manually editing many files, making the repo-wide formatting sweep consistent.
`git ls-files -z` plus `read -rd ''` is robust for tracked paths with whitespace or unusual characters.
The script only touches tracked files, avoiding generated or ignored build artifacts.
The check uses the final byte rather than pattern matching file contents, so it directly targets the exact POSIX-style final-newline issue.
The PR isolates formatting-only churn from semantic decomp work, making future functional PRs cleaner to review.
decomp_lessons: Keep decompilation source, headers, assembly stubs, and docs ending in a final newline to avoid noisy diffs unrelated to matching or symbol work.
Repository hygiene sweeps should be isolated in their own PR so future code movement, matching, and naming changes are not buried under formatting noise.
A small tracked maintenance script can make style invariants reproducible for future contributors.
Whitespace-only normalization matters in decomp projects because assembly, headers, and generated/context tooling often produce large diffs where noise makes review harder.
naming_conventions: No new decomp symbol naming conventions were introduced.
Existing file and symbol names visible in the diff, such as `ftCaptain_*`, `GX*`, `OS*`, `HSD_*`, and `func_########`, were not materially changed.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: No assembly matching tactic or codegen trick was introduced.
Assembly files were included only for final-newline normalization, including `asm/melee/ft/chara/ftNess/ftNess_data.s`, `asm/melee/lb/lbrefract_shared_001.s`, and the empty `asm/melee/ft/ftlib.s`.
For future matching work, reuse the lesson of separating whitespace normalization from semantic assembly/source edits.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or reviews were present in the provided PR context.
The PR merged quickly on the same day it was opened, but no explicit reviewer rationale is available in the slice.
searchable_terms: No newline at end of file
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that modify tools/newlines.sh or add CI/style checks for final newlines.
Search for future diffs reintroducing `No newline at end of file` markers.
Check whether contributor docs later mention running tools/newlines.sh before submitting formatting-heavy PRs.
